response:
## Decision: adopt O2 — gate on checker fidelity before the full grid

All three advisors independently scored O2 at 5 and every other option at 2-3, with zero spread — no second round was even triggered. That's not a close call: ISSUE-109.md itself already pre-registers this exact sequencing (checker-fidelity test before any full-grid spend), and ADR 0035's 25% false-accept measurement is the precise threat the issue names as the thing to gate on.

I wrote up the full record in **`docs/decisions/0036-issue-109-routing-checker-gate.md`** (5,100/6,000 chars). Summary:

- **Do**: fork `benchmarks/2026-07-10-tiered-execution/`'s harness to test checker false-accept rate across variants (Sonnet judge vs. checklist-scored deterministic) against the ~10-15% bar. Only run the full haiku/checker/sonnet-escalation grid if a variant clears it.
- **Don't**: run the full grid now (O1) — it burns the expensive escalation cells before knowing any checker is fit for purpose. Don't decline outright (O3) — routing is mechanistically distinct from the already-rejected coach-and-retry design, and the 25% figure was measured against a different validator task. Don't defer the gate itself (O4) — timing being the owner's call applies to the *adopt* decision, not to a cheap, immediately-runnable gate.
- **Falsifiable criteria**: whether checker-fidelity is actually cheaply separable from the full grid (if not, this collapses toward O1), and the outcome of the gate itself — clear ~10-15% → proceed to full grid under this same ADR; stay above it → record the null and close #109 with Sonnet-solo as the standing default.

I did not touch `ISSUE-109.md` itself — per ADR 0021, work-state stays in the issue and gets closed/updated by whoever executes the gate; this ADR is the decision-state artifact. Note: `adr-lint.mjs` requires shell approval I couldn't obtain in this session, so I verified the frontmatter/citation/falsifiability/budget rules by hand against the script's source — worth a real lint run before merge.

options.label: Run full routing experiment now
options.summary: Execute the complete grid across checker variants immediately, reusing the benchmarks/2026-07-10-tiered-execution harness as described.

options.label: Gate on checker fidelity first
options.summary: Test checker false-accept rate first; only proceed to the full grid if a checker variant beats ~10-15% false-accept.

options.label: Decline the experiment
options.summary: Do not run the routing experiment; keep Sonnet-solo as the default given the pre-registered 25% false-accept threat.

options.label: Defer with no urgency
options.summary: Postpone the decision to a later time, per the issue's own timing note that it is the owner's call with no urgency.

O1.crux: Running the full grid before checking checker fidelity spends the expensive Sonnet-escalation cells even though the issue itself says a sub-10-15% false-accept checker is a precondition for routing to beat Sonnet-solo, and #41 already measured the only checker tried (Sonnet judge) at 25% false-accept.
O1.dependency: If a cheap pilot showing checker false-accept rate is infeasible to isolate from the full grid (i.e., checker fidelity can only be measured as a byproduct of running everything), skipping straight to O1 stops being wasteful and becomes the only way to get the fidelity number at all.

O2.crux: The issue's own design note prescribes this order — compare checker variants against the 10-15% false-accept bar before any full-grid adopt decision — and #41's ADR 0035 already shows the one checker tested (Sonnet judge) missed that bar at 25% (6/24), so testing checker fidelity first is both pre-registered and cheap relative to the full grid.
O2.dependency: If cheap checker-fidelity-only cells cannot be decoupled from the full escalation grid (e.g. false-accept rate can only be scored after full Sonnet-redo comparison), the gate adds a step without saving spend, weakening the case for sequencing.

O3.crux: Declining outright discards a hypothesis the issue frames as cheap to test (harness reuse, ~$0.016-0.055/cell) and mechanistically distinct from #41's rejected coaching design, on the strength of a false-accept number measured against a *different* proposal (iterative critique) rather than against routing's single-shot checker.
O3.dependency: If the 25% false-accept rate is checker-invariant (i.e., no checker design — Sonnet judge, checklist, deterministic — can plausibly beat 10-15%), then declining without spending on a doomed gate is actually correct and this score should rise.

O4.crux: The issue explicitly states timing is the owner's call with no urgency and Sonnet-solo remains the safe default meanwhile, so deferring is never wrong on urgency grounds alone, but it also doesn't resolve the judgment call CLAUDE.md says must go through /decide now rather than sit unactioned.
O4.dependency: If there is an implicit deadline or opportunity cost to leaving #109 open (e.g. blocking other roadmap decisions or an owner request to close it this cycle), deferring stops being cost-free and the score drops.

O1.crux: Full grid skips the checker-fidelity gate the issue itself pre-registers as the required first step before adopt-relevant spend.
O1.dependency: Reverses if a checker variant's false-accept rate were already known (from prior data) to clear ~10-15%, making a separate gating step redundant — but no such evidence exists yet, only the 25% validator prior (ADR 0035) pointing the other way.

O2.crux: This is exactly the design the issue itself specifies (test checker false-accept before any full grid) and it directly targets the mechanism (25% false-accept, ADR 0035) that sank the closely related tiered config.
O2.dependency: Reverses if isolating checker fidelity actually costs nearly as much as the full grid (i.e., the two phases aren't cleanly separable) — then staging buys no savings and O1 becomes equally reasonable.

O3.crux: Declining outright discards a cheap, separable test of a mechanism (routing bounded by best-model-ships) that is meaningfully different from the already-rejected coach-and-iterate config, based only on a threat that hasn't been measured under routing's own checker.
O3.dependency: Reverses if routing's accept/escalate checker is functionally the same task as ADR 0035's final-artifact validator (so the 25% false-accept prior transfers directly) — then the cheap gate is foregone conclusion and declining is justified without spend.

O4.crux: The issue explicitly frames timing as owner's call with no urgency and Sonnet-solo stays the safe default meanwhile, so pure deferral costs nothing in the near term.
O4.dependency: Reverses if the checker-fidelity gate really is as cheap and fast as the issue claims ('cheap to test' section) — then deferring forfeits near-free information for no offsetting benefit, making O2 strictly dominant.

O1.crux: Running the whole grid up front spends the full ~0.016+E*(checker+0.055)/cell budget across all checker variants before knowing any variant clears the fidelity bar the issue itself pre-registers as the gate.
O1.dependency: If the harness can't cheaply isolate a checker-only fidelity phase (i.e. 'full grid' and 'gated grid' cost the same to execute), skipping the gate costs nothing extra and O1 collapses into O2.

O2.crux: Matches the issue's own design (test checker false-accept before the full grid) and ADR 0024's cheap-gate-before-spend doctrine, directly targeting the pre-registered 25% false-accept threat from #41's validator measurement.
O2.dependency: If no checker variant can be cheaply isolated/measured for false-accept rate independent of running full escalation cells, the gate adds a step without saving spend and O1 becomes equally justified.

O3.crux: Routing is mechanistically distinct from the rejected coach-and-retry config (bounded by whichever model ships, not iterated to Haiku's ceiling), so declining outright forecloses a test ADR 0035's own revisit triggers don't cover and that is cheap to gate first.
O3.dependency: If checker fidelity is effectively certain to fail (e.g. strong prior the 25% Sonnet-validator false-accept rate generalizes to any feasible checker), a cheap gate is pre-decided and decline-now is the efficient equivalent of running it.

O4.crux: Deferral is faithful to the issue's own 'owner's call, no urgency' note and keeps Sonnet-solo as the safe default, but it punts the decision this issue was explicitly opened to trigger rather than resolving it.
O4.dependency: If there is unstated urgency (e.g. a roadmap item or cost pressure depending on a routing verdict soon), deferral becomes the wrong call and the decision should proceed now via O2.

artifacts.docs/decisions/0036-issue-109-routing-ch:
---
id: 0036
title: "Issue #109 routing experiment: gate on checker fidelity before the full grid"
status: accepted
summary: "Adopt O2 — test checker false-accept rate first (bar ~10-15%) on the reused benchmarks/2026-07-10-tiered-execution harness; only run the full routing grid (haiku attempts, checker accepts/escalates whole task to sonnet) if a variant clears the bar. Reject O1 (full grid now), O3 (decline outright), O4 (defer the gate itself)."
---

# 0036 — issue #109 routing experiment: gate checker fidelity first

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM (judgment call routed through `/decide` per CLAUDE.md's "Feedback = PDCA trigger")
- Panel: 3 advisors, single round — no option's score spread reached 2, so no re-estimation round fired
- Context: ISSUE-109.md proposes "routing" (Haiku attempts once; checker accepts or escalates the whole task to Sonnet, no coaching) as the cascade shape #41/ADR 0035's rejected coach-and-retry design left untested. ADR 0035 measured the one checker tried so far (Sonnet judge, as final-artifact validator) at 25% false-accept (6/24); ISSUE-109.md:14,16 itself pre-registers ~10-15% false-accept as the bar a checker must clear before routing can beat Sonnet-solo, and names checker-fidelity-first as the required first step.

## Decision
Adopt **O2**: test checker false-accept rate first — compare checker variants (e.g. Sonnet judge vs. checklist-scored deterministic checks) against the ~10-15% false-accept bar, reusing `benchmarks/2026-07-10-tiered-execution/`'s 8 gradient tasks and hermetic harness. Only proceed to the full routing grid (haiku-solo + checker + sonnet-escalation cells, cost model 0.016 + E×(checker+0.055) per ISSUE-109.md:10) if a variant clears the bar. If none clears it, record the null per ADR 0024's record+plan discipline without spending the full grid. Finalize the adopt decision rule (frac-met non-inferior to Sonnet-solo at -0.05 margin, clustered CI, cost materially below) per ADR 0024 before any spend, as ISSUE-109.md:16 already sketches.

## Justification
All 3 advisors scored O2 at 5 and O1/O3/O4 at 2-3 — the issue's own design already pre-registers this order (ISSUE-109.md:12-14), and it targets the exact mechanism (25% false-accept) that sank the closely-related tiered config (ADR 0035). Every advisor's crux converges on the same cost argument: the gate is cheap relative to the full grid because it reuses an existing hermetic harness and adds only checker-variant cells, so spending the escalation cells before checking fidelity risks the ADR 0024 waste this repo already rejected once (ADR 0035). Declining outright (O3) discards a mechanistically distinct, cheap-to-gate hypothesis on a threat measured against a different design (iterative critique, not single-shot routing); deferring (O4) forfeits near-free information the issue itself says is available now — timing is the owner's call, but that licenses postponing the *adopt* decision, not skipping a cheap gate that is available immediately.

## Assumptions
- [checkable] a checker-fidelity-only phase is cheaply separable from the full escalation grid (every advisor's stated reversal condition) — owner: whoever forks the benchmark for this experiment; result: pending, verify before running any cells — if false, this ADR should be revised in place toward O1 (the gate would add a step without saving spend).
- [unverifiable] the 25% false-accept rate is checker-invariant (no feasible checker design beats 10-15%) — REOPEN-IF a checker variant's measured false-accept rate clears ~10-15%, proceed to the full grid per this Decision; if all variants stay above it, record the null and treat O3 (decline, keep Sonnet-solo default) as the terminal state.
- [checkable-doc] ISSUE-109.md's cost figures (0.016/0.055 per cell, 25%/10-15% false-accept) match ADR 0035's committed results — verified: ADR 0035 body states haiku 0.36x cost, sonnet baseline, and 6/24 (25%) false-accept from the parallel replication run.

## Rejected alternatives
- O1 (full grid now) — spends every escalation cell before knowing any checker clears the pre-registered fidelity bar; collapses to equivalent-to-O2 only if checker fidelity can't be isolated, which is unverified and contradicts the issue's own design.
- O3 (decline outright) — discards a mechanistically distinct, cheap hypothesis on a threat measured against a different (already-rejected) design's validator, not routing's own single-shot checker.
- O4 (defer, no urgency) — timing is genuinely the owner's call, but that applies to the *adopt* decision, not to a gate step that is cheap and runnable now; deferring the gate itself forfeits free information for no stated benefit.

## Revisit triggers
- Checker-fidelity phase turns out not isolable from the full grid at materially lower cost -> re-decide as O1-equivalent.
- A checker variant clears ~10-15% false-accept -> proceed to the full grid per this Decision; no new ADR needed.
- All checker variants stay above ~10-15% false-accept -> record the null (ADR 0024), keep Sonnet-solo default (O3), close #109.
